Executes a given bash command in a persistent shell session with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

Be aware: OS: linux, Shell: bash

All commands run in the current working directory by default. Use the `workdir` parameter if you need to run a command in a different directory. AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>` patterns - use `workdir` instead.

Use `/tmp/opencode` for temporary work outside the workspace. This directory has already been created, already exists, and is pre-approved for external directory access.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc. DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching, finding files) - use the specialized tools for this instead.

Before executing the command, please follow these steps:

1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

2. Command Execution:
   - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes (e.g., rm "path with spaces/file.txt")
   - Examples of proper quoting:
     - mkdir "/Users/name/My Documents" (correct)
     - mkdir /Users/name/My Documents (incorrect - will fail)
     - python "/path/with spaces/script.py" (correct)
     - python /path/with spaces/script.py (incorrect - will fail)
   - After ensuring proper quoting, execute the command.
   - Capture the output of the command.

Usage notes:
  - The command argument is required.
  - You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds. If not specified, commands will time out after 120000ms (2 minutes).
  - It is very helpful if you write a clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words.
  - If the output exceeds 2000 lines or 51200 bytes, it will be truncated and the full output will be written to a file. You can use Read with offset/limit to read specific sections or Grep to search the full content. Do NOT use `head`, `tail`, or other truncation commands to limit output; the full output will already be captured to a file for more precise searching.

  - Avoid using Bash with the `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or when these commands are truly necessary for the task. Instead, always prefer using the dedicated tools for these commands:
    - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
    - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
    - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
    - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
    - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
    - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
  - When issuing multiple commands:
    - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple Bash tool calls in a single message. For example, if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two Bash tool calls in parallel.
    - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together (e.g., `git add . && git commit -m "message" && git push`). For instance, if one operation must complete before another starts (like mkdir before cp, Write before Bash for git operations, or git add before git commit), run these operations sequentially instead.
    - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail
    - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings)
  - AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>`. Use the `workdir` parameter to change directories instead.
    <good-example>
    Use workdir="/foo/bar" with command: pytest tests
    </good-example>
    <bad-example>
    cd /foo/bar && pytest tests

# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- Avoid git commit --amend. ONLY use --amend when ALL conditions are met:
  (1) User explicitly requested amend, OR the commit succeeded and pre-commit hooks auto-modified files that need including — verify by checking `git log` that HEAD is the new commit before amending
  (2) HEAD commit was created by you in this conversation (verify: git log -1 --format='%an %ae')
  (3) Commit has NOT been pushed to remote (verify: git status shows "Your branch is ahead")
- CRITICAL: If commit FAILED or was REJECTED by hook, NEVER amend - fix the issue and create a NEW commit
- CRITICAL: If you already pushed to remote, NEVER amend unless user explicitly requests it (requires force push)
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.

1. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc.). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (see amend rules above)

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Task tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit

2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request summary
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands in parallel:

Use this tool to create and manage a structured task list for your current coding session. This helps you track progress, organize complex tasks, and demonstrate thoroughness to the user.
It also helps the user understand the progress of the task and overall progress of their requests.

## When to Use This Tool
Use this tool proactively in these scenarios:

1. Complex multistep tasks - When a task requires 3 or more distinct steps or actions
2. Non-trivial and complex tasks - Tasks that require careful planning or multiple operations
3. User explicitly requests todo list - When the user directly asks you to use the todo list
4. User provides multiple tasks - When users provide a list of things to be done (numbered or comma-separated)
5. After receiving new instructions - Immediately capture user requirements as todos. Feel free to edit the todo list based on new information.
6. After completing a task - Mark it complete and add any new follow-up tasks
7. When you start working on a new task, mark the todo as in_progress. Ideally you should only have one todo as in_progress at a time. Complete existing tasks before starting new ones.

## When NOT to Use This Tool

Skip using this tool when:
1. There is only a single, straightforward task
2. The task is trivial and tracking it provides no organizational benefit
3. The task can be completed in less than 3 trivial steps
4. The task is purely conversational or informational

NOTE that you should not use this tool if there is only one trivial task to do. In this case you are better off just doing the task directly.

## Examples of When to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: I want to add a dark mode toggle to the application settings. Make sure you run the tests and build when you're done!
Assistant: I'll help add a dark mode toggle to your application settings. Let me create a todo list to track this implementation.
*Creates todo list with the following items:*
1. Create dark mode toggle component in Settings page
2. Add dark mode state management (context/store)
3. Implement CSS-in-JS styles for dark theme
4. Update existing components to support theme switching
5. Run tests and build process, addressing any failures or errors that occur
*Begins working on the first task*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. Adding dark mode is a multi-step feature requiring UI, state management, and styling changes
2. The user explicitly requested tests and build be run afterward
3. The assistant inferred that tests and build need to pass by adding "Ensure tests and build succeed" as the final task
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Help me rename the function getCwd to getCurrentWorkingDirectory across my project
Assistant: Let me first search through your codebase to find all occurrences of 'getCwd'.
*Uses grep or search tools to locate all instances of getCwd in the codebase*
Assistant: I've found 15 instances of 'getCwd' across 8 different files. Let me create a todo list to track these changes.
*Creates todo list with specific items for each file that needs updating*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant searched to understand the scope of the task
2. Upon finding multiple occurrences across different files, it determined this was a complex task with multiple steps
3. The todo list helps ensure every instance is tracked and updated systematically
4. This approach prevents missing any occurrences and maintains code consistency
</reasoning>
</example>


<example>
User: I need to implement these features for my e-commerce site: user registration, product catalog, shopping cart, and checkout flow.
Assistant: I'll help implement these features. First, let's add all the features to the todo list.
*Creates a todo list breaking down each feature into specific tasks based on the project architecture*
Assistant: Let's start with user registration. This will involve creating database models, API endpoints, and frontend forms that integrate with your existing authentication system.

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. The user provided multiple complex features to implement in a comma separated list
2. The todo list helps organize these large features into manageable tasks
3. This approach allows for tracking progress across the entire implementation
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you help optimize my React application? It's rendering slowly and has performance issues.</user>
Assistant: I'll help optimize your React application. First, let me examine your codebase to identify potential performance bottlenecks.
*Reviews component structure, render patterns, state management, and data fetching*
Assistant: After analyzing your codebase, I've identified several performance issues. Let me create a todo list to track our optimization efforts.
*Creates todo list with items like: 1) Implement memoization for expensive calculations in ProductList, 2) Add virtualization for long lists in Dashboard, 3) Optimize image loading in Gallery component, 4) Fix state update loops in ShoppingCart, 5) Review bundle size and implement code splitting*
Let's start by implementing memoization for the expensive calculations in your ProductList component.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant examined the codebase to identify specific performance issues
2. Based on this analysis, it identified multiple optimization opportunities
3. Performance optimization is a non-trivial task requiring multiple steps
4. The todo list helps methodically track improvements across different components
5. This systematic approach ensures all performance bottlenecks are addressed
</reasoning>
</example>

## Examples of When NOT to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: How do I print 'Hello World' in Python?
Assistant: In Python, you can print "Hello World" with this simple code:

python
print("Hello World")

This will output the text "Hello World" to the console when executed.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, trivial task that can be completed in one step. There's no need to track multiple tasks or steps for such a straightforward request.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: What does the git status command do?
Assistant: The git status command shows the current state of your working directory and staging area. It displays which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git. This is a fundamental command for understanding the current state of your repository before making commits.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is an informational request with no actual coding task to complete. The user is simply asking for an explanation, not for the assistant to perform multiple steps or tasks.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does?
Assistant: Sure, let me add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does.
* Uses the Edit tool to add a comment to the calculateTotal function *

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, straightforward task confined to one location in the code. Adding a comment doesn't require tracking multiple steps or systematic organization.

Assistant: I'll run the npm install command for you.

The command completed successfully. Here's the output:

All dependencies have been installed according to your package.json file.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single command execution with immediate results. There are no multiple steps to track or organize, making the todo list unnecessary for this straightforward task.
</reasoning>
</example>

## Task States and Management

1. **Task States**: Use these states to track progress:
   - pending: Task not yet started
   - in_progress: Currently working on (limit to ONE task at a time)
   - completed: Task finished successfully
   - cancelled: Task no longer needed

2. **Task Management**:
   - Update task status in real-time as you work
   - Mark tasks complete IMMEDIATELY after finishing (don't batch completions)
   - Only have ONE task in_progress at any time
   - Complete current tasks before starting new ones
   - Cancel tasks that become irrelevant

3. **Task Breakdown**:
   - Create specific, actionable items
   - Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
   - Use clear, descriptive task names

When in doubt, use this tool. Being proactive with task management demonstrates attentiveness and ensures you complete all requirements successfully.

data.content:
# Write Cypher

## Overview

Use this skill for VAST DB Cypher query patterns. Prefer reusable templates, and construct new queries when no template matches the analysis need.

## Workflow

1. Read `references/query-templates.md`. If a template matches the task, reuse it as-is.
2. If no template matches, construct a new Cypher query using the VAST DB schema and the closest related template as guidance.

## Query Strategy

- Load `understand-mlir-schema` when query construction or result interpretation depends on schema details.
- Use small exploratory queries with `LIMIT` when schema details such as operation names, properties, node and relationship labels are uncertain.
- When starting from a source location, first map it to operation `uid`s. Use those `uid`s as stable handles for follow-up queries such as enclosing functions, call relationships, and def-use data flow.
- Return only properties needed for the analysis. Common fields are node `uid`, relationship `idx`, operation `name`, `location`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `className`, type `type`, and attribute `value`.
- Order indexed relationships by `idx`.

## Query Rules

- Generate read-only queries by default. Do not use `CREATE`, `MERGE`, `SET`, `DELETE`, or `REMOVE`.
- Use parameter placeholders such as `$func_name`, `$op_uid`, `$file_name`, `$line`, and `$callee`.
- Do not invent node and relationship labels, properties, or operation names. Verify unfamiliar schema details with `understand-mlir-schema` or exploratory queries.

## Common Patterns

Read `references/query-templates.md` for reusable Cypher templates.

Use this before writing Cypher queries against VAST DB. For query patterns, load write-cypher.

# Understand MLIR Schema

## Overview

This skill describes the VAST DB schema. Use it to check node labels, relationship types, and properties before writing or interpreting Cypher queries. Do not guess labels or relationships; look them up here.

## Core Schema

### Node Labels

- `OPERATIONS` — MLIR operations. Properties include `uid`, `name`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `mod_name`, and `location`. The `location` field has the form `['F', file, line, column]`.
- `VALUES` — SSA values. Only property is `uid`.
- `INTATTRS` — Integer attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (int), `attr_type` (int).
- `FLOATATTRS` — Float attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (float), `attr_type` (int).
- `STRINGATTRS` — String attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (string), `attr_type` (int).
- `TYPES` — MLIR types. Properties: `uid`, `type` (type mnemonic), `has_dl` (bool), `bw` (int bitwidth), `align` (int), `record_name` (string, for hl.record only).
- `REGIONS` — MLIR regions. Properties: `uid`, `regType` (bool: true for GraphRegion, false for SSACFG).
- `BLOCKS` — MLIR blocks. Only property is `uid`.
- `VTABLES` — Virtual tables. Properties: `uid`, `vtable_name`, `components_flat`, `components_sizes`.
- `VTTS` — VTT (virtual table table). Properties: `uid`, `vtt_name`, `components_flat`.

### Key Relationships

Operation operands and results:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation consumes a value as operand
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:RESVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation produces a value as result
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGATTR {idx}]->(:INTATTRS|FLOATATTRS|STRINGATTRS)` — operation has an attribute
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — operation references a block (e.g., branch targets)
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGTYPE {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — operation has a type operand

Containment (operation bodies):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:CHILDREGION {idx}]->(:REGIONS)` — operation contains a region
- `(:REGIONS)-[:CHILDBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — region contains a block
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:CHILDINST {idx}]->(:OPERATIONS)` — block contains an instruction (operation)
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:BLOCKARG {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — block has a block argument

Type system:
- `(:VALUES)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — value has a type
- `(:TYPES)-[:ARG {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — composite type has component types
- `(:INTATTRS|:FLOATATTRS)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — non-string attribute has a type

Function and variable types:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:FUNCTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global function's type signature
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:VARTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global variable's type

Virtual tables (only from `builtin.module` operations):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTABLE]->(:VTABLES)` — module has a vtable
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTT]->(:VTTS)` — module has a VTT

## References

- `references/schema-mlir-graph.md` — canonical node labels, relationships, properties, constraints, and example queries.
- `references/operations-catalog.md` — every operation name (`hl.func`, `hl.call`, `ll.load`, etc.) with its specific properties and relationships.
- `references/types-catalog.md` — every type mnemonic (`hl.int`, `i32`, `core.fn`, etc.) with its properties and subtype structure.

In process_relocs:   - At line 1447: the check `if (!do_reloc && !do_got_section_contents) return true;` - if do_got_section_contents is true, we don't return   - At line 1568 (else branch): since do_using_dynamic is likely false   - At line 1574: section = filedata->section_headers (which is NULL)   - At line 1578: display_relocations is called with section = NULL

In display_relocations at line 1318: section->sh_type causes NULL pointer dereferenceThe bug is that when section_headers fails to load:- do_reloc is set to false- But the code doesn't check if section_headers is NULL before using it- The early return check at line 1447 is insufficient because it only returns if BOTH do_reloc AND do_got_section_contents are false. If do_got_section_contents is true (e.g., due to process_got_section_contents at line 1699 being called before process_relocs), the code continues and tries to iterate over NULL section_headers.

## Root Cause

The vulnerability is a NULL pointer dereference in the `display_relocations` function at line 1318 where `section->sh_type` is accessed. The `section` pointer becomes NULL when `filedata->section_headers` is NULL. This occurs because:

1. In `process_object`, `process_section_headers` can fail (returning false), which sets `do_reloc = false` but leaves `filedata->section_headers` as NULL
2. In `process_relocs`, the early return check at line 1447 (`if (!do_reloc && !do_got_section_contents) return true;`) only returns if **both** flags are false
3. If `do_got_section_contents` is true (e.g., set by earlier processing), the function continues despite `section_headers` being NULL
4. At line 1574, the loop sets `section = filedata->section_headers` (NULL)
5. At line 1578, `display_relocations` is called with a NULL section pointer
6. At line 1318, accessing `section->sh_type` triggers the NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476)

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:1614` `process_object`
   - Main entry point that processes an ELF file; calls `process_section_headers` which can fail, leaving `section_headers` as NULL

2. `bad.c:1656` `process_section_headers` (call)
   - Called to load section headers from the ELF file; if it fails, `do_reloc` is set to false at line 1662 but `section_headers` remains NULL

3. `bad.c:1673` `process_relocs` (call)
   - Called to process relocation sections; the early return at line 1447 is bypassed when `do_got_section_contents` is true

4. `bad.c:1574` (in `process_relocs`)
   - Loop initialization: `section = filedata->section_headers` - obtains NULL since section loading failed

5. `bad.c:1578` `display_relocations` (call)
   - Called with NULL `section` pointer from the loop in `process_relocs`

6. `bad.c:1318` `display_relocations`
   - Vulnerable line: `relocation_type rel_type = rel_type_from_sh_type (section->sh_type);` - dereferences NULL pointer `section` to access `sh_type`

460: static bool
461: get_32bit_section_headers (Filedata * filedata, bool probe)
462: {
463:   Elf32_External_Shdr * shdrs;
464:   Elf_Internal_Shdr *   internal;
465:   unsigned int          i;
466:   unsigned int          size = filedata->file_header.e_shentsize;
467:   unsigned int          num = probe ? 1 : filedata->file_header.e_shnum;
468: 
469:   /* PR binutils/17531: Cope with unexpected section header sizes.  */
470:   if (size == 0 || num == 0)
471:     return false;
472: 
473:   /* The section header cannot be at the start of the file - that is
474:      where the ELF file header is located.  A file with absolutely no
475:      sections in it will use a shoff of 0.  */
476:   if (filedata->file_header.e_shoff == 0)
477:     return false;
478: 
479:   if (size < sizeof * shdrs)
480:     {
481:       if (! probe)
482: 	error (_("The e_shentsize field in the ELF header is less than the size of an ELF section header\n"));
483:       return false;
484:     }
485:   if (!probe && size > sizeof * shdrs)
486:     warn (_("The e_shentsize field in the ELF header is larger than the size of an ELF section header\n"));
487: 
488:   shdrs = (Elf32_External_Shdr *) get_data (NULL, filedata, filedata->file_header.e_shoff,
489:                                             size, num,
490: 					    probe ? NULL : _("section headers"));
491:   if (shdrs == NULL)
492:     return false;
493: 
494:   filedata->section_headers = (Elf_Internal_Shdr *)
495:     cmalloc (num, sizeof (Elf_Internal_Shdr));
496:   if (filedata->section_headers == NULL)
497:     {
498:       if (!probe)
499: 	error (_("Out of memory reading %u section headers\n"), num);
500:       free (shdrs);
501:       return false;
502:     }
503: 
504:   for (i = 0, internal = filedata->section_headers;
505:        i < num;
506:        i++, internal++)
507:     {
508:       internal->sh_name      = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_name);
509:       internal->sh_type      = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_type);
510:       internal->sh_flags     = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_flags);
511:       internal->sh_addr      = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_addr);
512:       internal->sh_offset    = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_offset);
513:       internal->sh_size      = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_size);
514:       internal->sh_link      = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_link);
515:       internal->sh_info      = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_info);
516:       internal->sh_addralign = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_addralign);
517:       internal->sh_entsize   = BYTE_GET (shdrs[i].sh_entsize);
518:       if (!probe && internal->sh_link > num)
519: 	warn (_("Section %u has an out of range sh_link value of %u\n"), i, internal->sh_link);
520:       if (!probe && internal->sh_flags & SHF_INFO_LINK && internal->sh_info > num)
521: 	warn (_("Section %u has an out of range sh_info value of %u\n"), i, internal->sh_info);
522:     }
523: 
524:   free (shdrs);
525:   return true;
526: }

598: static bool
599: get_section_headers (Filedata *filedata, bool probe)
600: {

602:     return true;

605:     return get_32bit_section_headers (filedata, probe);
606:   else
607:     return get_64bit_section_headers (filedata, probe);

610: static bool
611: process_section_headers (Filedata * filedata)
612: {
613:   Elf_Internal_Shdr * section;
614:   unsigned int i;
615: 
616:   if (filedata->file_header.e_shnum == 0)
617:     {
618:       /* PR binutils/12467.  */
619:       if (filedata->file_header.e_shoff != 0)
620: 	{
621: 	  warn (_("possibly corrupt ELF file header - it has a non-zero"
622: 		  " section header offset, but no section headers\n"));
623: 	  return false;
624: 	}
625:       else if (do_sections)
626: 	printf (_("\nThere are no sections in this file.\n"));
627: 
628:       return true;
629:     }
630: 
631:   if (do_sections && !do_header)
632:     {
633:       if (filedata->is_separate && process_links)
634: 	printf (_("In linked file '%s': "),
635: 		printable_string (filedata->file_name, 0));
636:       if (! filedata->is_separate || process_links)
637: 	printf (ngettext ("There is %d section header, "
638: 			  "starting at offset %#" PRIx64 ":\n",
639: 			  "There are %d section headers, "
640: 			  "starting at offset %#" PRIx64 ":\n",
641: 			  filedata->file_header.e_shnum),
642: 		filedata->file_header.e_shnum,
643: 		filedata->file_header.e_shoff);
644:     }
645: 
646:   if (!get_section_headers (filedata, false))
647:     return false;
648: 
649:   /* Read in the string table, so that we have names to display.  */
650:   if (filedata->string_table == NULL
651:       && filedata->file_header.e_shstrndx != SHN_UNDEF
652:       && filedata->file_header.e_shstrndx < filedata->file_header.e_shnum)
653:     {
654:       section = filedata->section_headers + filedata->file_header.e_shstrndx;
655: 
656:       if (section->sh_size != 0)
657: 	{
658: 	  filedata->string_table = (char *) get_data (NULL, filedata, section->sh_offset,
659: 						      1, section->sh_size,
660: 						      _("string table"));
661: 
662: 	  filedata->string_table_length = filedata->string_table != NULL ? section->sh_size : 0;
663: 	}
664:     }
665: 
666:   /* Scan the sections for the dynamic symbol table
667:      and dynamic string table and debug sections.  */
668:   eh_addr_size = is_32bit_elf ? 4 : 8;
669:   switch (filedata->file_header.e_machine)
670:     {
671:     case EM_MIPS:
672:     case EM_MIPS_RS3_LE:
673:       /* The 64-bit MIPS EABI uses a combination of 32-bit ELF and 64-bit
674: 	 FDE addresses.  However, the ABI also has a semi-official ILP32
675: 	 variant for which the normal FDE address size rules apply.
676: 
677: 	 GCC 4.0 marks EABI64 objects with a dummy .gcc_compiled_longXX
678: 	 section, where XX is the size of longs in bits.  Unfortunately,
679: 	 earlier compilers provided no way of distinguishing ILP32 objects
680: 	 from LP64 objects, so if there's any doubt, we should assume that
681: 	 the official LP64 form is being used.  */
682:       if ((filedata->file_header.e_flags & EF_MIPS_ABI) == EF_MIPS_ABI_EABI64
683: 	  && find_section (filedata, ".gcc_compiled_long32") == NULL)
684: 	eh_addr_size = 8;
685:       break;
686: 
687:     case EM_H8_300:
688:     case EM_H8_300H:
689:       switch (filedata->file_header.e_flags & EF_H8_MACH)
690: 	{
691: 	case E_H8_MACH_H8300:
692: 	case E_H8_MACH_H8300HN:
693: 	case E_H8_MACH_H8300SN:
694: 	case E_H8_MACH_H8300SXN:
695: 	  eh_addr_size = 2;
696: 	  break;
697: 	case E_H8_MACH_H8300H:
698: 	case E_H8_MACH_H8300S:
699: 	case E_H8_MACH_H8300SX:
700: 	  eh_addr_size = 4;
701: 	  break;
702: 	}
703:       break;
704: 
705:     case EM_M32C_OLD:
706:     case EM_M32C:
707:       switch (filedata->file_header.e_flags & EF_M32C_CPU_MASK)
708: 	{
709: 	case EF_M32C_CPU_M16C:
710: 	  eh_addr_size = 2;
711: 	  break;
712: 	}
713:       break;
714:     }
715: 
716: #define CHECK_ENTSIZE_VALUES(section, i, size32, size64)		\
717:   do									\
718:     {									\
719:       uint64_t expected_entsize = is_32bit_elf ? size32 : size64;	\
720:       if (section->sh_entsize != expected_entsize)			\
721: 	{								\
722: 	  error (_("Section %d has invalid sh_entsize of %" PRIx64 "\n"), \
723: 		 i, section->sh_entsize);				\
724: 	  error (_("(Using the expected size of %" PRIx64 " for the rest of this dump)\n"), \
725: 		 expected_entsize);					\
726: 	  section->sh_entsize = expected_entsize;			\
727: 	}								\
728:     }									\
729:   while (0)
730: 
731: #define CHECK_ENTSIZE(section, i, type)					\
732:   CHECK_ENTSIZE_VALUES (section, i, sizeof (Elf32_External_##type),	\
733: 			sizeof (Elf64_External_##type))
734: 
735:   for (i = 0, section = filedata->section_headers;
736:        i < filedata->file_header.e_shnum;
737:        i++, section++)
738:     {
739:       const char *name = printable_section_name (filedata, section);
740: 
741:       /* Run some sanity checks on the headers and
742: 	 possibly fill in some file data as well.  */
743:       switch (section->sh_type)
744: 	{
745: 	case SHT_DYNSYM:
746: 	  if (filedata->dynamic_symbols != NULL)
747: 	    {
748: 	      error (_("File contains multiple dynamic symbol tables\n"));
749: 	      continue;
750: 	    }
751: 
752: 	  CHECK_ENTSIZE (section, i, Sym);
753: 	  filedata->dynamic_symbols
754: 	    = get_elf_symbols (filedata, section, &filedata->num_dynamic_syms);
755: 	  filedata->dynamic_symtab_section = section;
756: 	  break;
757: 
758: 	case SHT_STRTAB:
759: 	  if (streq (name, ".dynstr"))
760: 	    {
761: 	      if (filedata->dynamic_strings != NULL)
762: 		{
763: 		  error (_("File contains multiple dynamic string tables\n"));
764: 		  continue;
765: 		}
766: 
767: 	      filedata->dynamic_strings
768: 		= (char *) get_data (NULL, filedata, section->sh_offset,
769: 				     1, section->sh_size, _("dynamic strings"));
770: 	      filedata->dynamic_strings_length
771: 		= filedata->dynamic_strings == NULL ? 0 : section->sh_size;
772: 	      filedata->dynamic_strtab_section = section;
773: 	    }
774: 	  break;
775: 
776: 	case SHT_SYMTAB_SHNDX:
777: 	  {
778: 	    elf_section_list * entry = xmalloc (sizeof * entry);
779: 
780: 	    entry->hdr = section;
781: 	    entry->next = filedata->symtab_shndx_list;
782: 	    filedata->symtab_shndx_list = entry;
783: 	  }
784: 	  break;
785: 
786: 	case SHT_SYMTAB:
787: 	  CHECK_ENTSIZE (section, i, Sym);
788: 	  break;
789: 
790: 	case SHT_GROUP:
791: 	  CHECK_ENTSIZE_VALUES (section, i, GRP_ENTRY_SIZE, GRP_ENTRY_SIZE);
792: 	  break;
793: 
794: 	case SHT_REL:
795: 	  CHECK_ENTSIZE (section, i, Rel);
796: 	  if (do_checks && section->sh_size == 0)
797: 	    warn (_("Section '%s': zero-sized relocation section\n"), name);
798: 	  break;
799: 
800: 	case SHT_RELA:
801: 	  CHECK_ENTSIZE (section, i, Rela);
802: 	  if (do_checks && section->sh_size == 0)
803: 	    warn (_("Section '%s': zero-sized relocation section\n"), name);
804: 	  break;
805: 
806: 	case SHT_RELR:
807: 	  CHECK_ENTSIZE (section, i, Relr);
808: 	  break;
809: 
810: 	case SHT_NOTE:
811: 	case SHT_PROGBITS:
812: 	case SHT_GNU_SFRAME:
813: 	  /* Having a zero sized section is not illegal according to the
814: 	     ELF standard, but it might be an indication that something
815: 	     is wrong.  So issue a warning if we are running in lint mode.  */
816: 	  if (do_checks && section->sh_size == 0)
817: 	    warn (_("Section '%s': has a size of zero - is this intended ?\n"), name);
818: 	  break;
819: 
820: 	default:
821: 	  break;
822: 	}
823: 
824:       if ((do_debugging || do_debug_info || do_debug_abbrevs
825: 	   || do_debug_lines || do_debug_pubnames || do_debug_pubtypes
826: 	   || do_debug_aranges || do_debug_frames || do_debug_macinfo
827: 	   || do_debug_str || do_debug_str_offsets || do_debug_loc
828: 	   || do_debug_ranges
829: 	   || do_debug_addr || do_debug_cu_index || do_debug_links)
830: 	  && (startswith (name, ".debug_")
831: 	      || startswith (name, ".zdebug_")))
832: 	{
833:           if (name[1] == 'z')
834:             name += sizeof (".zdebug_") - 1;
835:           else
836:             name += sizeof (".debug_") - 1;
837: 
838: 	  if (do_debugging
839: 	      || (do_debug_info     && startswith (name, "info"))
840: 	      || (do_debug_info     && startswith (name, "types"))
841: 	      || (do_debug_abbrevs  && startswith (name, "abbrev"))
842: 	      || (do_debug_lines    && strcmp (name, "line") == 0)
843: 	      || (do_debug_lines    && startswith (name, "line."))
844: 	      || (do_debug_pubnames && startswith (name, "pubnames"))
845: 	      || (do_debug_pubtypes && startswith (name, "pubtypes"))
846: 	      || (do_debug_pubnames && startswith (name, "gnu_pubnames"))
847: 	      || (do_debug_pubtypes && startswith (name, "gnu_pubtypes"))
848: 	      || (do_debug_aranges  && startswith (name, "aranges"))
849: 	      || (do_debug_ranges   && startswith (name, "ranges"))
850: 	      || (do_debug_ranges   && startswith (name, "rnglists"))
851: 	      || (do_debug_frames   && startswith (name, "frame"))
852: 	      || (do_debug_macinfo  && startswith (name, "macinfo"))
853: 	      || (do_debug_macinfo  && startswith (name, "macro"))
854: 	      || (do_debug_str      && startswith (name, "str"))
855: 	      || (do_debug_links    && startswith (name, "sup"))
856: 	      || (do_debug_str_offsets && startswith (name, "str_offsets"))
857: 	      || (do_debug_loc      && startswith (name, "loc"))
858: 	      || (do_debug_loc      && startswith (name, "loclists"))
859: 	      || (do_debug_addr     && startswith (name, "addr"))
860: 	      || (do_debug_cu_index && startswith (name, "cu_index"))
861: 	      || (do_debug_cu_index && startswith (name, "tu_index"))
862: 	      )
863: 	    request_dump_bynumber (&filedata->dump, i, DEBUG_DUMP);
864: 	}
865:       /* Linkonce section to be combined with .debug_info at link time.  */
866:       else if ((do_debugging || do_debug_info)
867: 	       && startswith (name, ".gnu.linkonce.wi."))
868: 	request_dump_bynumber (&filedata->dump, i, DEBUG_DUMP);
869:       else if (do_debug_frames && streq (name, ".eh_frame"))
870: 	request_dump_bynumber (&filedata->dump, i, DEBUG_DUMP);
871:       else if (do_debug_frames && streq (name, ".eh_frame_hdr"))
872: 	request_dump_bynumber (&filedata->dump, i, DEBUG_DUMP);
873:       else if (do_gdb_index && (streq (name, ".gdb_index")
874: 				|| streq (name, ".debug_names")))
875: 	request_dump_bynumber (&filedata->dump, i, DEBUG_DUMP);
876:       /* Trace sections for Itanium VMS.  */
877:       else if ((do_debugging || do_trace_info || do_trace_abbrevs
878:                 || do_trace_aranges)
879: 	       && startswith (name, ".trace_"))
880: 	{
881:           name += sizeof (".trace_") - 1;
882: 
883: 	  if (do_debugging
884: 	      || (do_trace_info     && streq (name, "info"))
885: 	      || (do_trace_abbrevs  && streq (name, "abbrev"))
886: 	      || (do_trace_aranges  && streq (name, "aranges"))
887: 	      )
888: 	    request_dump_bynumber (&filedata->dump, i, DEBUG_DUMP);
889: 	}
890:       else if ((do_debugging || do_debug_links)
891: 	       && (startswith (name, ".gnu_debuglink")
892: 		   || startswith (name, ".gnu_debugaltlink")))
893: 	request_dump_bynumber (&filedata->dump, i, DEBUG_DUMP);
894:     }
895: 
896:   if (! do_sections)
897:     return true;
898: 
899:   if (filedata->is_separate && ! process_links)
900:     return true;
901: 
902:   if (filedata->is_separate)
903:     printf (_("\nSection Headers in linked file '%s':\n"),
904: 	    printable_string (filedata->file_name, 0));
905:   else if (filedata->file_header.e_shnum > 1)
906:     printf (_("\nSection Headers:\n"));
907:   else
908:     printf (_("\nSection Header:\n"));
909: 
910:   if (is_32bit_elf)
911:     {
912:       if (do_section_details)
913: 	{
914: 	  printf (_("  [Nr] Name\n"));
915: 	  printf (_("       Type            Addr     Off    Size   ES   Lk Inf Al\n"));
916: 	}
917:       else
918: 	printf
919: 	  (_("  [Nr] Name              Type            Addr     Off    Size   ES Flg Lk Inf Al\n"));
920:     }
921:   else if (do_wide)
922:     {
923:       if (do_section_details)
924: 	{
925: 	  printf (_("  [Nr] Name\n"));
926: 	  printf (_("       Type            Address          Off    Size   ES   Lk Inf Al\n"));
927: 	}
928:       else
929: 	printf
930: 	  (_("  [Nr] Name              Type            Address          Off    Size   ES Flg Lk Inf Al\n"));
931:     }
932:   else
933:     {
934:       if (do_section_details)
935: 	{
936: 	  printf (_("  [Nr] Name\n"));
937: 	  printf (_("       Type              Address          Offset            Link\n"));
938: 	  printf (_("       Size              EntSize          Info              Align\n"));
939: 	}
940:       else
941: 	{
942: 	  printf (_("  [Nr] Name              Type             Address           Offset\n"));
943: 	  printf (_("       Size              EntSize          Flags  Link  Info  Align\n"));
944: 	}
945:     }
946: 
947:   if (do_section_details)
948:     printf (_("       Flags\n"));
949: 
950:   for (i = 0, section = filedata->section_headers;
951:        i < filedata->file_header.e_shnum;
952:        i++, section++)
953:     {
954:       /* Run some sanity checks on the section header.  */
955: 
956:       /* Check the sh_link field.  */
957:       switch (section->sh_type)
958: 	{
959: 	case SHT_REL:
960: 	case SHT_RELR:
961: 	case SHT_RELA:
962: 	  if (section->sh_link == 0
963: 	      && (filedata->file_header.e_type == ET_EXEC
964: 		  || filedata->file_header.e_type == ET_DYN))
965: 	    /* A dynamic relocation section where all entries use a
966: 	       zero symbol index need not specify a symtab section.  */
967: 	    break;
968: 	  /* Fall through.  */
969: 	case SHT_SYMTAB_SHNDX:
970: 	case SHT_GROUP:
971: 	case SHT_HASH:
972: 	case SHT_GNU_HASH:
973: 	case SHT_GNU_versym:
974: 	  if (section->sh_link == 0
975: 	      || section->sh_link >= filedata->file_header.e_shnum
976: 	      || (filedata->section_headers[section->sh_link].sh_type != SHT_SYMTAB
977: 		  && filedata->section_headers[section->sh_link].sh_type != SHT_DYNSYM))
978: 	    warn (_("[%2u]: Link field (%u) should index a symtab section.\n"),
979: 		  i, section->sh_link);
980: 	  break;
981: 
982: 	case SHT_DYNAMIC:
983: 	case SHT_SYMTAB:
984: 	case SHT_DYNSYM:
985: 	case SHT_GNU_verneed:
986: 	case SHT_GNU_verdef:
987: 	case SHT_GNU_LIBLIST:
988: 	  if (section->sh_link == 0
989: 	      || section->sh_link >= filedata->file_header.e_shnum
990: 	      || filedata->section_headers[section->sh_link].sh_type != SHT_STRTAB)
991: 	    warn (_("[%2u]: Link field (%u) should index a string section.\n"),
992: 		  i, section->sh_link);
993: 	  break;
994: 
995: 	case SHT_INIT_ARRAY:
996: 	case SHT_FINI_ARRAY:
997: 	case SHT_PREINIT_ARRAY:
998: 	  if (section->sh_type < SHT_LOOS && section->sh_link != 0)
999: 	    warn (_("[%2u]: Unexpected value (%u) in link field.\n"),
1000: 		  i, section->sh_link);
1001: 	  break;
1002: 
1003: 	default:
1004: 	  /* FIXME: Add support for target specific section types.  */
1005: #if 0 	  /* Currently we do not check other section types as there are too
1006: 	     many special cases.  Stab sections for example have a type
1007: 	     of SHT_PROGBITS but an sh_link field that links to the .stabstr
1008: 	     section.  */
1009: 	  if (section->sh_type < SHT_LOOS && section->sh_link != 0)
1010: 	    warn (_("[%2u]: Unexpected value (%u) in link field.\n"),
1011: 		  i, section->sh_link);
1012: #endif
1013: 	  break;
1014: 	}
1015: 
1016:       /* Check the sh_info field.  */
1017:       switch (section->sh_type)
1018: 	{
1019: 	case SHT_REL:
1020: 	case SHT_RELA:
1021: 	  if (section->sh_info == 0
1022: 	      && (filedata->file_header.e_type == ET_EXEC
1023: 		  || filedata->file_header.e_type == ET_DYN))
1024: 	    /* Dynamic relocations apply to segments, so they do not
1025: 	       need to specify the section they relocate.  */
1026: 	    break;
1027: 	  if (section->sh_info == 0
1028: 	      || section->sh_info >= filedata->file_header.e_shnum
1029: 	      || (filedata->section_headers[section->sh_info].sh_type != SHT_PROGBITS
1030: 		  && filedata->section_headers[section->sh_info].sh_type != SHT_NOBITS
1031: 		  && filedata->section_headers[section->sh_info].sh_type != SHT_NOTE
1032: 		  && filedata->section_headers[section->sh_info].sh_type != SHT_INIT_ARRAY
1033: 		  && filedata->section_headers[section->sh_info].sh_type != SHT_FINI_ARRAY
1034: 		  && filedata->section_headers[section->sh_info].sh_type != SHT_PREINIT_ARRAY
1035: 		  /* FIXME: Are other section types valid ?  */
1036: 		  && filedata->section_headers[section->sh_info].sh_type < SHT_LOOS))
1037: 	    warn (_("[%2u]: Info field (%u) should index a relocatable section.\n"),
1038: 		  i, section->sh_info);
1039: 	  break;
1040: 
1041: 	case SHT_DYNAMIC:
1042: 	case SHT_HASH:
1043: 	case SHT_SYMTAB_SHNDX:
1044: 	case SHT_INIT_ARRAY:
1045: 	case SHT_FINI_ARRAY:
1046: 	case SHT_PREINIT_ARRAY:
1047: 	  if (section->sh_info != 0)
1048: 	    warn (_("[%2u]: Unexpected value (%u) in info field.\n"),
1049: 		  i, section->sh_info);
1050: 	  break;
1051: 
1052: 	case SHT_GROUP:
1053: 	case SHT_SYMTAB:
1054: 	case SHT_DYNSYM:
1055: 	  /* A symbol index - we assume that it is valid.  */
1056: 	  break;
1057: 
1058: 	default:
1059: 	  /* FIXME: Add support for target specific section types.  */
1060: 	  if (section->sh_type == SHT_NOBITS)
1061: 	    /* NOBITS section headers with non-zero sh_info fields can be
1062: 	       created when a binary is stripped of everything but its debug
1063: 	       information.  The stripped sections have their headers
1064: 	       preserved but their types set to SHT_NOBITS.  So do not check
1065: 	       this type of section.  */
1066: 	    ;
1067: 	  else if (section->sh_flags & SHF_INFO_LINK)
1068: 	    {
1069: 	      if (section->sh_info < 1 || section->sh_info >= filedata->file_header.e_shnum)
1070: 		warn (_("[%2u]: Expected link to another section in info field"), i);
1071: 	    }
1072: 	  else if (section->sh_type < SHT_LOOS
1073: 		   && (section->sh_flags & SHF_GNU_MBIND) == 0
1074: 		   && section->sh_info != 0)
1075: 	    warn (_("[%2u]: Unexpected value (%u) in info field.\n"),
1076: 		  i, section->sh_info);
1077: 	  break;
1078: 	}
1079: 
1080:       /* Check the sh_size field.  */
1081:       if (section->sh_size > filedata->file_size
1082: 	  && section->sh_type != SHT_NOBITS
1083: 	  && section->sh_type != SHT_NULL
1084: 	  && section->sh_type < SHT_LOOS)
1085: 	warn (_("Size of section %u is larger than the entire file!\n"), i);
1086: 
1087:       printf ("  [%2u] ", i);
1088:       if (do_section_details)
1089: 	printf ("%s\n      ", printable_section_name (filedata, section));
1090:       else
1091: 	print_symbol_name (-17, printable_section_name (filedata, section));
1092: 
1093:       printf (do_wide ? " %-15s " : " %-15.15s ",
1094: 	      get_section_type_name (filedata, section->sh_type));
1095: 
1096:       if (is_32bit_elf)
1097: 	{
1098: 	  const char * link_too_big = NULL;
1099: 
1100: 	  print_vma (section->sh_addr, LONG_HEX);
1101: 
1102: 	  printf ( " %6.6lx %6.6lx %2.2lx",
1103: 		   (unsigned long) section->sh_offset,
1104: 		   (unsigned long) section->sh_size,
1105: 		   (unsigned long) section->sh_entsize);
1106: 
1107: 	  if (do_section_details)
1108: 	    fputs ("  ", stdout);
1109: 	  else
1110: 	    printf (" %3s ", get_elf_section_flags (filedata, section->sh_flags));
1111: 
1112: 	  if (section->sh_link >= filedata->file_header.e_shnum)
1113: 	    {
1114: 	      link_too_big = "";
1115: 	      /* The sh_link value is out of range.  Normally this indicates
1116: 		 an error but it can have special values in Solaris binaries.  */
1117: 	      switch (filedata->file_header.e_machine)
1118: 		{
1119: 		case EM_386:
1120: 		case EM_IAMCU:
1121: 		case EM_X86_64:
1122: 		case EM_L1OM:
1123: 		case EM_K1OM:
1124: 		case EM_OLD_SPARCV9:
1125: 		case EM_SPARC32PLUS:
1126: 		case EM_SPARCV9:
1127: 		case EM_SPARC:
1128: 		  if (section->sh_link == (SHN_BEFORE & 0xffff))
1129: 		    link_too_big = "BEFORE";
1130: 		  else if (section->sh_link == (SHN_AFTER & 0xffff))
1131: 		    link_too_big = "AFTER";
1132: 		  break;
1133: 		default:
1134: 		  break;
1135: 		}
1136: 	    }
1137: 
1138: 	  if (do_section_details)
1139: 	    {
1140: 	      if (link_too_big != NULL && * link_too_big)
1141: 		printf ("<%s> ", link_too_big);
1142: 	      else
1143: 		printf ("%2u ", section->sh_link);
1144: 	      printf ("%3u %2lu\n", section->sh_info,
1145: 		      (unsigned long) section->sh_addralign);
1146: 	    }
1147: 	  else
1148: 	    printf ("%2u %3u %2lu\n",
1149: 		    section->sh_link,
1150: 		    section->sh_info,
1151: 		    (unsigned long) section->sh_addralign);
1152: 
1153: 	  if (link_too_big && ! * link_too_big)
1154: 	    warn (_("section %u: sh_link value of %u is larger than the number of sections\n"),
1155: 		  i, section->sh_link);
1156: 	}
1157:       else if (do_wide)
1158: 	{
1159: 	  print_vma (section->sh_addr, LONG_HEX);
1160: 
1161: 	  if ((long) section->sh_offset == section->sh_offset)
1162: 	    printf (" %6.6lx", (unsigned long) section->sh_offset);
1163: 	  else
1164: 	    {
1165: 	      putchar (' ');
1166: 	      print_vma (section->sh_offset, LONG_HEX);
1167: 	    }
1168: 
1169: 	  if ((unsigned long) section->sh_size == section->sh_size)
1170: 	    printf (" %6.6lx", (unsigned long) section->sh_size);
1171: 	  else
1172: 	    {
1173: 	      putchar (' ');
1174: 	      print_vma (section->sh_size, LONG_HEX);
1175: 	    }
1176: 
1177: 	  if ((unsigned long) section->sh_entsize == section->sh_entsize)
1178: 	    printf (" %2.2lx", (unsigned long) section->sh_entsize);
1179: 	  else
1180: 	    {
1181: 	      putchar (' ');
1182: 	      print_vma (section->sh_entsize, LONG_HEX);
1183: 	    }
1184: 
1185: 	  if (do_section_details)
1186: 	    fputs ("  ", stdout);
1187: 	  else
1188: 	    printf (" %3s ", get_elf_section_flags (filedata, section->sh_flags));
1189: 
1190: 	  printf ("%2u %3u ", section->sh_link, section->sh_info);
1191: 
1192: 	  if ((unsigned long) section->sh_addralign == section->sh_addralign)
1193: 	    printf ("%2lu\n", (unsigned long) section->sh_addralign);
1194: 	  else
1195: 	    {
1196: 	      print_vma (section->sh_addralign, DEC);
1197: 	      putchar ('\n');
1198: 	    }
1199: 	}
1200:       else if (do_section_details)
1201: 	{
1202: 	  putchar (' ');
1203: 	  print_vma (section->sh_addr, LONG_HEX);
1204: 	  if ((long) section->sh_offset == section->sh_offset)
1205: 	    printf ("  %16.16lx", (unsigned long) section->sh_offset);
1206: 	  else
1207: 	    {
1208: 	      printf ("  ");
1209: 	      print_vma (section->sh_offset, LONG_HEX);
1210: 	    }
1211: 	  printf ("  %u\n       ", section->sh_link);
1212: 	  print_vma (section->sh_size, LONG_HEX);
1213: 	  putchar (' ');
1214: 	  print_vma (section->sh_entsize, LONG_HEX);
1215: 
1216: 	  printf ("  %-16u  %lu\n",
1217: 		  section->sh_info,
1218: 		  (unsigned long) section->sh_addralign);
1219: 	}
1220:       else
1221: 	{
1222: 	  putchar (' ');
1223: 	  print_vma (section->sh_addr, LONG_HEX);
1224: 	  if ((long) section->sh_offset == section->sh_offset)
1225: 	    printf ("  %8.8lx", (unsigned long) section->sh_offset);
1226: 	  else
1227: 	    {
1228: 	      printf ("  ");
1229: 	      print_vma (section->sh_offset, LONG_HEX);
1230: 	    }
1231: 	  printf ("\n       ");
1232: 	  print_vma (section->sh_size, LONG_HEX);
1233: 	  printf ("  ");
1234: 	  print_vma (section->sh_entsize, LONG_HEX);
1235: 
1236: 	  printf (" %3s ", get_elf_section_flags (filedata, section->sh_flags));
1237: 
1238: 	  printf ("     %2u   %3u     %lu\n",
1239: 		  section->sh_link,
1240: 		  section->sh_info,
1241: 		  (unsigned long) section->sh_addralign);
1242: 	}
1243: 
1244:       if (do_section_details)
1245: 	{
1246: 	  printf ("       %s\n", get_elf_section_flags (filedata, section->sh_flags));
1247: 	  if ((section->sh_flags & SHF_COMPRESSED) != 0)
1248: 	    {
1249: 	      /* Minimum section size is 12 bytes for 32-bit compression
1250: 		 header + 12 bytes for compressed data header.  */
1251: 	      unsigned char buf[24];
1252: 
1253: 	      assert (sizeof (buf) >= sizeof (Elf64_External_Chdr));
1254: 	      if (get_data (&buf, filedata, section->sh_offset, 1,
1255: 			    sizeof (buf), _("compression header")))
1256: 		{
1257: 		  Elf_Internal_Chdr chdr;
1258: 
1259: 		  if (get_compression_header (&chdr, buf, sizeof (buf)) == 0)
1260: 		    printf (_("       [<corrupt>]\n"));
1261: 		  else
1262: 		    {
1263: 		      if (chdr.ch_type == ch_compress_zlib)
1264: 			printf ("       ZLIB, ");
1265: 		      else if (chdr.ch_type == ch_compress_zstd)
1266: 			printf ("       ZSTD, ");
1267: 		      else
1268: 			printf (_("       [<unknown>: 0x%x], "),
1269: 				chdr.ch_type);
1270: 		      print_vma (chdr.ch_size, LONG_HEX);
1271: 		      printf (", %lu\n", (unsigned long) chdr.ch_addralign);
1272: 		    }
1273: 		}
1274: 	    }
1275: 	}
1276:     }
1277: 
1278:   if (!do_section_details)
1279:     {
1280:       /* The ordering of the letters shown here matches the ordering of the
1281: 	 corresponding SHF_xxx values, and hence the order in which these
1282: 	 letters will be displayed to the user.  */
1283:       printf (_("Key to Flags:\n\
1284:   W (write), A (alloc), X (execute), M (merge), S (strings), I (info),\n\
1285:   L (link order), O (extra OS processing required), G (group), T (TLS),\n\
1286:   C (compressed), x (unknown), o (OS specific), E (exclude),\n  "));
1287:       switch (filedata->file_header.e_ident[EI_OSABI])
1288: 	{
1289: 	case ELFOSABI_GNU:
1290: 	case ELFOSABI_FREEBSD:
1291: 	  printf (_("R (retain), "));
1292: 	  /* Fall through */
1293: 	case ELFOSABI_NONE:
1294: 	  printf (_("D (mbind), "));
1295: 	  break;
1296: 	default:
1297: 	  break;
1298: 	}
1299:       if (filedata->file_header.e_machine == EM_X86_64
1300: 	  || filedata->file_header.e_machine == EM_L1OM
1301: 	  || filedata->file_header.e_machine == EM_K1OM)
1302: 	printf (_("l (large), "));
1303:       else if (filedata->file_header.e_machine == EM_ARM)
1304: 	printf (_("y (purecode), "));
1305:       else if (filedata->file_header.e_machine == EM_PPC)
1306: 	printf (_("v (VLE), "));
1307:       printf ("p (processor specific)\n");
1308:     }
1309: 
1310:   return true;
1311: }

1313: static bool
1314: display_relocations (Elf_Internal_Shdr *  section,
1315: 		     Filedata *           filedata,
1316: 		     bool                 dump_reloc)
1317: {
1318:   relocation_type rel_type = rel_type_from_sh_type (section->sh_type);
1319: 
1320:   if (rel_type == reltype_unknown)
1321:     return false;
1322: 
1323:   uint64_t rel_size = section->sh_size;
1324: 
1325:   if (rel_size == 0)
1326:     return false;
1327: 
1328:   if (dump_reloc)
1329:     {
1330:       if (filedata->is_separate)
1331: 	printf (_("\nIn linked file '%s' relocation section "),
1332: 		printable_string (filedata->file_name, 0));
1333:       else
1334: 	printf (_("\nRelocation section "));
1335: 
1336:       if (filedata->string_table == NULL)
1337: 	printf ("%d", section->sh_name);
1338:       else
1339: 	printf ("'%s'", printable_section_name (filedata, section));
1340:     }
1341: 
1342:   uint64_t rel_offset = section->sh_offset;
1343:   uint64_t num_rela = rel_size / section->sh_entsize;
1344:   uint64_t num_reloc;
1345:   uint64_t *relrs = NULL;
1346: 
1347:   if (rel_type == reltype_relr)
1348:     {
1349:       /* Just stating the 'number of entries' in a RELR section can be
1350: 	 misleading, since this is not the number of locations relocated, but
1351: 	 the number of words in the compressed RELR format.  So also provide
1352: 	 the number of locations affected.  */
1353: 
1354:       num_reloc = count_relr_relocations (filedata, section, &relrs);
1355: 
1356:       if (dump_reloc)
1357: 	{
1358: 	  printf (_(" at offset %#" PRIx64), rel_offset);
1359: 	  printf (ngettext (" contains %" PRIu64 " entry which relocates",
1360: 			    " contains %" PRIu64 " entries which relocate",
1361: 			    num_rela), num_rela);
1362: 	  printf (ngettext (" %" PRIu64 " location:\n",
1363: 			    " %" PRIu64 " locations:\n",
1364: 			    num_reloc), num_reloc);
1365: 	}
1366:     }
1367:   else
1368:     {
1369:       num_reloc = num_rela;
1370:       if (dump_reloc)
1371: 	printf (ngettext (" at offset %#" PRIx64
1372: 			  " contains %" PRIu64 " entry:\n",
1373: 			  " at offset %#" PRIx64
1374: 			  " contains %" PRIu64 " entries:\n",
1375: 			  num_rela),
1376: 		rel_offset, num_rela);
1377:     }
1378: 
1379:   update_all_relocations (num_reloc);
1380: 
1381:   Elf_Internal_Shdr * symsec;
1382:   Elf_Internal_Sym *  symtab = NULL;
1383:   uint64_t            nsyms = 0;
1384:   uint64_t            strtablen = 0;
1385:   char *              strtab = NULL;
1386: 
1387:   if (section->sh_link == 0
1388:       || section->sh_link >= filedata->file_header.e_shnum)
1389:     {
1390:       /* Symbol data not available.
1391: 	 This can happen, especially with RELR relocs.
1392: 	 See if there is a .symtab section present.
1393: 	 If so then use it.  */
1394:       symsec = find_section_by_name (filedata, ".symtab");
1395:     }
1396:   else
1397:     {
1398:       symsec = filedata->section_headers + section->sh_link;
1399: 
1400:       if (symsec->sh_type != SHT_SYMTAB
1401: 	  && symsec->sh_type != SHT_DYNSYM)
1402: 	{
1403: 	  free (relrs);
1404: 	  return false;
1405: 	}
1406:     }
1407: 
1408:   if (symsec != NULL
1409:       && !get_symtab (filedata, symsec, &symtab, &nsyms, &strtab, &strtablen))
1410:     {
1411:       free (relrs);
1412:       return false;
1413:     }
1414: 
1415:   bool res;
1416: 
1417:   if (rel_type == reltype_relr)
1418:     {
1419:       res = dump_relr_relocations (filedata, section->sh_size,
1420: 				   section->sh_entsize,
1421: 				   section->sh_offset,
1422: 				   relrs,
1423: 				   symtab, nsyms, strtab, strtablen,
1424: 				   dump_reloc);
1425:       free (relrs);
1426:     }
1427:   else
1428:     res = dump_relocations (filedata, rel_offset, rel_size,
1429: 			    symtab, nsyms, strtab, strtablen,
1430: 			    rel_type,
1431: 			    symsec == NULL
1432: 			    ? false : symsec->sh_type == SHT_DYNSYM,
1433: 			    dump_reloc);
1434:   free (strtab);
1435:   free (symtab);
1436: 
1437:   return res;
1438: }

1440: static bool
1441: process_relocs (Filedata * filedata)
1442: {
1443:   uint64_t rel_size;
1444:   uint64_t rel_offset;
1445:   unsigned int rel_entsz;
1446: 
1447:   if (!do_reloc && !do_got_section_contents)
1448:     return true;
1449: 
1450:   if (do_using_dynamic)
1451:     {
1452:       relocation_type rel_type;
1453:       const char * name;
1454:       const char * entsz_name;
1455:       bool  has_dynamic_reloc;
1456:       unsigned int i;
1457: 
1458:       has_dynamic_reloc = false;
1459: 
1460:       for (i = 0; i < ARRAY_SIZE (dynamic_relocations); i++)
1461: 	{
1462: 	  rel_size = filedata->dynamic_info[dynamic_relocations [i].size];
1463: 
1464: 	  if (!rel_size)
1465: 	    continue;
1466: 
1467: 	  has_dynamic_reloc = true;
1468: 
1469: 	  rel_type = dynamic_relocations [i].rel_type;
1470: 	  name = dynamic_relocations [i].name;
1471: 	  rel_offset = filedata->dynamic_info[dynamic_relocations [i].reloc];
1472: 
1473: 	  if (rel_type == reltype_unknown)
1474: 	    {
1475: 	      if (dynamic_relocations [i].reloc != DT_JMPREL)
1476: 		abort ();
1477: 	      switch (filedata->dynamic_info[DT_PLTREL])
1478: 		{
1479: 		case DT_REL:
